variables.content:
For the homogeneous unpolarized two-dimensional electron gas in a finite square cell, adding one electron with momentum \mathbf{p} (|\mathbf{p}|>k_F) to the N-particle ground state produces a finite-size correction to the total kinetic-energy contribution of the excitation given by
      \delta T_{\mathbf{p}}^{N}=\frac{\hbar^{2}\mathbf{p}^{2}}{2m}\;Z_{\mathbf{p}}^{N}\big[e^{-\Delta_{N}}-1\big],
      where Z_{\mathbf{p}}^{N} is the finite-N quasiparticle weight at momentum \mathbf{p} and \Delta_{N} is the missing-small-q integral defined in conclusion 1. Because \Delta_{N}\sim N^{-1/4} for large N, the leading finite-size error in the near-k_F excitation dispersion used to extract the single-particle effective mass m^{*} (via the linearized relation \varepsilon_{\mathbf{k}}\approx\hbar^{2}k_F(k-k_F)/m^{*}) also scales as N^{-1/4}; therefore, extrapolations that assume faster vanishing finite-size errors can give qualitatively incorrect conclusions about the sign and magnitude of m^{*}-m for typical accessible system sizes.

For an added-electron excitation of momentum \mathbf{p} with |\mathbf{p}|>k_F in the homogeneous two-dimensional electron gas, the leading finite-size error in the excitation energy arising from the change in the momentum distribution is dominated by the kinetic-energy term
      \delta T_{\mathbf p}^N=\frac{\hbar^2 p^2}{2m}\,Z_{\mathbf p}^N\big[e^{-\Delta_N}-1\big],
      where Z_{\mathbf p}^N is the finite-N quasiparticle weight at \mathbf{p} and \Delta_N is the missing-small-q integral defined above; in leading order the corresponding potential-energy correction is independent of \mathbf{p} and therefore does not affect the slope of the excitation dispersion used to extract the effective mass m^{*} from near-k_F fits.Eq. (12)

Let the two-layer effective mass formula be M = μ4 / [ vF2 C1 ] * sqrt( 1 + C1 / μ3 ), with μ = δ1 + δ2 and C1 = 2 γ N2 (ℏ vF)2 where γ = e2 / [ 8π ε0 εd D3 ]. Because γ ∝ D−3 enters multiplicatively into C1, the effective mass M increases as interlayer separation D increases within this formula; this dependence on D arises from the quasi-relativistic (Dirac-like) kinetic structure used to derive the dispersion rather than from a nonrelativistic Schrödinger description, in which the exciton mass does not acquire such a D-dependence.

The proposition: For Fermi liquid parameters $F_l$ (or $F_l/m^{*}$) of the two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) computed in square periodic simulation cells containing $N$ electrons, the leading systematic finite-size correction arising from long-range correlation effects scales asymptotically as $N^{\gamma}$ with exponent $\gamma=-1/4$ (i.e., a correction term proportional to $N^{-1/4}$), so that finite-$N$ results can be extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting to the form $F_l(N)=c+aN^{-1/4}$; this scaling is the same as that argued in the theoretical analyses of long-range-correlation finite-size effects (Holzmann et al.).

The claim that finite-width corrections to dispersion decrease with increasing in-plane magnetic momentum P is given a physical rationale (that the lateral size of the magnetoexciton grows with P and reduces sensitivity to vertical structure), but the paper does not present explicit quantitative plots that isolate finite-width corrections as functions of P (for example, side-by-side comparisons of strictly 2D versus quasi-2D dispersions as functions of P for representative sets of D and d) that would demonstrate the monotonic decrease in the correction with P.

Fits of the measured plasmon dispersion using a model that incorporates the finite strip width and electron exchange-correlation yield an effective electron mass $m^{*}\approx0.5\,m_e$ (where $m_e$ is the free-electron mass) and an effective two-dimensional electron density $N_{2D}\approx8\times10^{13}\ \text{cm}^{-2}$; averaging this areal density over the strip width $L\approx4\ \text{nm}$ gives an equivalent one-dimensional density $N_{1D}\approx N_{2D}\times L\approx3.2\times10^{7}\ \text{cm}^{-1}$, and the initial, almost-linear portion of the lowest-plasmon dispersion corresponds to a plasmon group velocity $v_{\text{PL}}\approx1\times10^{6}\ \text{m}\,\text{s}^{-1}$.

For a MoS2 monolayer embedded in the thin heterostructure environments considered (isolated, on a single hBN layer, or sandwiched between two hBN layers), many-body quasiparticle corrections to the single-particle band curvatures (and hence to the electron effective mass m_e^* and hole effective mass m_h^*) and substrate-induced renormalization of those band curvatures are assumed to be negligible for the purpose of computing the exciton reduced mass μ_ex, so that the ground-state DFT/LDA value μ_ex = 0.27 m_e (with m_e the free-electron mass) is used without applying GW-type corrections or explicit substrate renormalization schemes.

For a two-dimensional electron gas the plasmon frequency at in-plane wave vector $q$, denoted $\omega_{p}(q)$, has the long-wavelength classical form $\omega_{cl}(q)=\omega_{0}\sqrt{q}$ with $\omega_{0}^{2}=2\pi n e^{2}/m$, where $n$ is the 2D electron density, $e$ is the elementary charge, and $m$ is the carrier effective mass; the leading-order nonlocal (finite-wave-vector) random-phase-approximation (RPA) correction to this classical dispersion is
      $$
      \frac{\omega_{p}(q)}{\omega_{cl}(q)} \;=\; 1 \;+\; \frac{3}{4}\frac{q}{q_{TF}},
      $$
      where the two-dimensional screening wave vector is $q_{TF}=g m e^{2}/\hbar^{2}$, $g$ is the degeneracy factor (spin multiplicity), and $\hbar$ is Planck's constant divided by $2\pi$; the symbol $k_{F}=(4\pi n/g)^{1/2}$ denotes the 2D Fermi wave vector. This RPA finite-$q$ correction increases the plasmon frequency above the classical value for finite $q$ and becomes quantitatively important when the wave vector $q$ is comparable to or larger than the Fermi wave vector $k_{F}$.

Define the kinetic energy per particle for a two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas computed from the momentum distribution $n_{\mathbf{k}}$ as
      $$
      T=\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m}\frac{1}{V}\sum_{\mathbf{k}}k^{2}n_{\mathbf{k}},
      $$
      where the sum over $\mathbf{k}$ runs over discrete periodic-system wavevectors in a cell of area $V=L^{2}$. The leading finite-size corrections to both the kinetic energy per particle and to the potential energy per particle, arising from finite-$N$ deviations of $n_{\mathbf{k}}$ that are localized near $k_{F}$ and governed by the same small-$q$ integrals that produce $\Delta_{N}\sim N^{-1/4}$, scale as $N^{-5/4}$ in two dimensions.

The standard small-$q$ plasmon dispersion relations for ideal homogeneous electron gases are taken in this analysis as
      $$
      \omega_{p}^{2}\approx a q+\frac{3}{4}\left(v_{F}q\right)^{2},
      $$
      for a strictly two-dimensional electron gas, and
      $$
      \omega_{p}^{2}\approx\frac{N e^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0}\kappa m^{*}}+\frac{3}{5}\left(v_{F}q\right)^{2},
      $$
      for a homogeneous three-dimensional electron gas, where in these expressions $\omega_{p}$ is the plasmon angular frequency, $q$ is the in-plane wavevector magnitude, $a=\dfrac{N^{2D}e^{2}}{2\varepsilon_{0}\kappa m^{*}}$ with $N^{2D}$ the two-dimensional areal electron density, $N$ the volumetric electron density, $e$ the elementary charge, $\varepsilon_{0}$ the vacuum permittivity, $\kappa$ the dielectric screening constant of the medium, $m^{*}$ the electron effective mass, and $v_{F}$ the Fermi velocity. It is assumed here that these homogeneous 2D and 3D dispersion formulae may be used without modification to interpret the optical long-wavelength ($q\approx0$) backscattered Raman response of a real multilayer structure consisting of nine silicon $\delta$-doped GaAs planes with finite interlayer spacing $d$ and finite top and spacer layer thicknesses, so that the presence or absence of a finite-frequency backscattered Raman $L^{+}$ mode can be interpreted directly as indicating predominantly two-dimensional versus quasi-three-dimensional collective character in the measured multilayer region.
